fault_hypothesis: The condition on line 70 uses strict greater-than (`> limit`) instead of greater-than-or-equal (`>= limit`), so when `len(active) == limit` the function incorrectly returns `True` with `remaining=0` instead of `False, 0`.

spec.expr: len(active__1) < limit or (len(active__1) == limit and __cbfl_result == (False, 0)) or (len(active__1) > limit and __cbfl_result == (False, 0))

constraints.intent: When active count equals or exceeds limit, the result must be (False, 0); the strict > misses the equality case.
In the else branch (allowed=True), active count must be strictly less than limit; equality should have been caught by the if.
Before the condition is evaluated, if active equals limit the guard should trigger denial, so this expression being false reveals the off-by-one boundary bug.
The returned allowed flag must be False whenever active count is at or above the limit.
